statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, sort the array in ascending order using the Selection Sort algorithm. The Selection Sort algorithm works by repeatedly finding the minimum element from the unsorted part of the array and swapping it with the first unsorted element. The algorithm maintains two subarrays in a given array: the subarray which is already sorted, and the remaining subarray which is unsorted. In every iteration of the selection sort, the minimum element from the unsorted subarray is picked and moved to the sorted subarray.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N space-separated integers, representing the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The output will be a single line containing N space-separated integers, representing the sorted array.
statement.notes: The input array will contain distinct integers.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The array is sorted in ascending order using the Selection Sort algorithm.
The array is sorted in ascending order using the Selection Sort algorithm.

hints.text: To start solving this problem, you should first understand the basics of the Selection Sort algorithm. It's an in-place comparison-based algorithm in which the list is divided into two parts, the sorted part at the left end and the unsorted part at the right end. Initially, the sorted part is empty and the unsorted part is the entire list.

The algorithm then iterates over the unsorted part of the list, finds the smallest (or largest, depending on the sorting order) element, and swaps it with the first element of the unsorted part. This process is repeated until the entire list is sorted. You should think about how to implement this process in your code, focusing on how to keep track of the sorted and unsorted parts of the list.

A key insight to solving this problem efficiently is recognizing that with each iteration, the size of the unsorted part of the list decreases by one, as one element is moved to the sorted part. You can use a loop to iterate over the list, and within each iteration, find the minimum element in the unsorted part and swap it with the first element of the unsorted part. Consider using two nested loops: one to control the number of passes through the list and another to find the minimum in the unsorted part during each pass.

editorial.title: Selection Sort: An Intuitive yet Inefficient Sorting Algorithm
editorial.intuition: The brute force approach to sorting an array involves comparing each element with every other element to find the smallest one and placing it at the beginning, which is the core idea behind selection sort. This method is straightforward and easy to implement but lacks efficiency for large datasets.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force method is correct but slow because it involves nested loops that compare each element with every other element, resulting in a time complexity of O(n^2). This makes it impractical for large arrays.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would involve optimizing the comparison process or using a more efficient sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution for sorting an array of integers would be to use a built-in sorting function or a more efficient sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort, which have average time complexities of O(n log n). However, for the specific case of selection sort, the provided reference solution is already optimal in terms of simplicity and ease of implementation, despite its O(n^2) time complexity.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for the selection sort algorithm lies in the fact that it iteratively selects the smallest element from the unsorted portion of the array and swaps it with the first element of the unsorted portion, effectively sorting the array in ascending order.
editorial.dryRun: To test the selection sort algorithm, one can create a sample array with a mix of positive and negative integers, run the algorithm, and verify that the output is a sorted array in ascending order.
commonMistakes: Failing to update the minimum index when a smaller element is found
Not swapping the minimum element with the first element of the unsorted portion
Using an incorrect loop range or condition
Not handling edge cases like an empty or single-element array
